findings.title: CheckIOAvailable uses a PID sourced from pg_stat_activity without bounds or liveness validation
findings.description: The tech-spec states that app.setup() queries pg_stat_activity to obtain 'first backend PID' and passes it to CheckIOAvailable(pid). The existing CheckIOAvailable implementation constructs the path /proc/<pid>/io directly from the integer and opens the file. There is no validation that the PID is positive, non-zero, or that it actually belongs to the expected PostgreSQL process before the path is opened. A race condition exists between the query and the open: the backend may exit between the two calls, causing pid reuse by an unrelated process — in this case the probe result (ioAvailable=true/false) is misleading. Additionally, pg_stat_activity may legitimately return a PID of 0 for certain backend types (autovacuum workers, walsender, etc.) on some PostgreSQL versions. A zero PID would open /proc/0/io, which on Linux refers to the swapper/idle task and is unreadable; the resulting EACCES or ENOENT would incorrectly mark IO unavailable. A negative or very large PID (from a corrupt query result) would construct a malformed path that filepath.Clean cannot fully sanitize.
findings.impact: Probe result may incorrectly be false (IO columns suppressed for the entire recording session) or true (columns enabled when not readable). On a system with PID reuse this is a correctness issue; it is not a privilege escalation vector because the recorder already runs as the same user as the pgcenter process, and procfs opens are sandboxed to that user's readable set.

findings.title: sysinfo JSON deserialization from tar has no size limit
findings.description: The tech-spec specifies that readTar handles sysinfo.* entries by calling json.Unmarshal on their content decoded via stat.NewPGresultFile(r, hdr.Size). The hdr.Size field comes from the tar header, which is under control of whoever created the tar file. A maliciously crafted tar with hdr.Size=math.MaxInt64 and a sysinfo entry containing a giant JSON body would cause the reader to allocate a very large buffer before json.Unmarshal is called. While pgcenter record writes these files itself and the typical use-case is a trusted local tar file, the report subcommand accepts an arbitrary -f path — a user may open a tar received from an untrusted source. SysInfo is only two numeric fields (ticks float64, cpu_count int), so a well-formed payload is at most ~50 bytes. There is no size cap enforced at the sysinfo branch in readTar.
findings.location: Section: Architecture — Modified components (report/report.go: readTar handles sysinfo.* entries)
findings.impact: Denial-of-service via memory exhaustion if an attacker can supply a crafted tar file to a user running pgcenter report -N. Requires local file delivery — not remotely exploitable.

findings.title: Silent discard of per-PID procfs errors during record collection
findings.description: The tech-spec states: 'Individual PID errors (process exited mid-tick, EACCES on /proc/[pid]/io) are skipped silently.' While this is the established TUI pattern, the record context is different: a multi-tick recording session should surface persistent EACCES errors (e.g. kernel security policy changed, or the recorder is running as a user that consistently cannot read /proc/<pid>/io) rather than silently producing empty IO columns across all captured snapshots. Without any log output, the user has no signal that IO data is missing from the recorded archive.
findings.location: Section: Architecture — Data flow (per-tick collect() block); Section: Decisions — Decision 8
findings.impact: A user replaying the archive with pgcenter report -N sees empty IO columns with no explanation. The root cause (EACCES, changed kernel configuration) is invisible. Not a direct security risk, but reduces observability of the security boundary around /proc/<pid>/io.

findings.title: GetSysticksLocal shells out to getconf — PATH-dependent execution
findings.description: The existing getSysticksLocal() (to be exported as GetSysticksLocal) uses exec.Command("getconf", "CLK_TCK"). exec.Command with a bare command name resolves the binary through PATH. If PATH is manipulated in the environment where pgcenter record runs, a crafted getconf binary could be executed instead of the system one. This risk already exists in the TUI code path (NewCollector), and the tech-spec correctly notes this (Risk table, row 3). The export makes this path reachable from one additional call site (app.setup()) without any additional hardening.
findings.location: Section: Decisions — Decision 3: Export GetSysticksLocal; internal/stat/stat.go:getSysticksLocal
findings.impact: If PATH is attacker-controlled (e.g., the binary is run via sudo with an inherited PATH, or in a compromised CI environment), a rogue getconf could return an arbitrary CLK_TCK value, causing all CPU rate columns in the recording to be wrong. This is a pre-existing risk, not introduced by this feature, but the export increases surface area.
findings.recommendation:
Use an absolute path for the getconf binary, or read CLK_TCK directly via syscall.SysctlRaw / C.sysconf(_SC_CLK_TCK) via cgo, or parse /proc/sys/kernel/hz. The simplest safe fix is to use the full path:

  exec.Command("/usr/bin/getconf", "CLK_TCK")

Alternatively, on Linux, CLK_TCK can be read from the auxiliary vector (AT_CLKTCK) without spawning a subprocess, which is both faster and PATH-independent. This fix should be applied to the existing unexported function before it is exported, so both call sites benefit.
